questions.question: What is the key difference between a message queue and pub/sub?
options: Queues are faster than pub/sub
A queue delivers each message to one consumer (load-balancing); pub/sub delivers each event to all subscribers (fan-out)
Pub/sub can only send text
There is no difference
questions.explanation: A queue distributes work — each message is handled once by one consumer. Pub/sub broadcasts — every subscriber of a topic gets its own copy of each event. Queues solve 'do this work once'; pub/sub solves 'many things react to this event'.

questions.question: In pub/sub, what does the publisher know about its subscribers?
options: It maintains a list of every subscriber and calls each one
Nothing — it publishes to a topic, and the broker delivers to whoever subscribed
Only the first subscriber
It must approve each subscriber
questions.explanation: The publisher is decoupled from subscribers: it emits an event to a topic without knowing who (if anyone) listens. The broker routes copies to all subscribers. This is why you can add a new reaction by adding a subscriber, with no change to the publisher.

questions.question: A 'user.signed_up' event should trigger email, billing, AND analytics independently. Which pattern fits?
options: A single queue with three workers sharing the load
Pub/sub fan-out, so each of the three services receives its own copy of the event
Direct synchronous calls only
A database trigger
questions.explanation: All three must react to the same event, so you need fan-out: pub/sub delivers a copy to each subscriber. A shared queue would give the event to only one of the three workers, so the other two reactions would never happen.

questions.question: What is the main downside of event-driven architecture compared to direct calls?
options: It cannot scale
The end-to-end flow is harder to see and debug — logic is spread across many independent subscribers with no single call stack
It only works on one machine
Events cannot carry data
questions.explanation: Decoupling comes at the cost of traceability. With direct calls you can read the flow top to bottom; with events the behavior is emergent across many independently-deployed reactions, so answering 'why did X happen?' means tracing events across services.

questions.question: Why is event-driven architecture more resilient to a subscriber being down?
options: Subscribers never go down
With a durable broker, the event waits and is processed when the subscriber recovers, while the original action still succeeded
The publisher retries synchronously until the subscriber is up
The event is discarded so nothing breaks
questions.explanation: Because the publisher doesn't call subscribers directly, a down subscriber doesn't fail the original action. A durable broker holds the event until the subscriber recovers and processes it. The order succeeds even if, say, the email service is temporarily offline.
